mechanism: Both increase bleeding risk; aspirin inhibits platelet aggregation while warfarin reduces clotting factor synthesis.
management: Avoid combination when possible. If concurrent use is indicated, use the lowest effective aspirin dose, monitor INR more frequently, watch for bleeding signs.

mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits warfarin metabolism (CYP2C9/CYP3A4), raising INR.
management: Empiric warfarin dose reduction by 30–50%; check INR within 1 week and again at 2 weeks.

mechanism: Serotonin syndrome — both increase synaptic serotonin.
management: Contraindicated. Allow at least 14-day washout when switching; 5 weeks for fluoxetine.

mechanism: Linezolid is a weak MAOI; combining with SSRI risks serotonin syndrome.
management: Avoid if possible. If used, monitor for serotonin syndrome (hyperthermia, clonus, agitation).

mechanism: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, the enzyme that activates clopidogrel — reduced antiplatelet effect.
management: Prefer pantoprazole or rabeprazole if PPI needed concurrently.

mechanism: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4; statin levels rise → rhabdomyolysis risk.
management: Hold statin during clarithromycin course (or switch to a non-CYP3A4 antibiotic).

mechanism: Acute kidney injury risk from contrast → reduced metformin clearance → lactic acidosis risk.
management: Hold metformin at time of contrast; restart 48h later after confirming normal renal function in at-risk patients.

mechanism: Both increase bleeding risk (NSAID gastric injury + warfarin anticoagulation).
management: Avoid chronic concurrent use. If short-term needed, gastroprotection + INR monitoring.

mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9 — warfarin metabolism slows → INR rises.
management: Reduce warfarin dose 25–50%; recheck INR within 3–5 days.

mechanism: Tramadol has serotonergic activity — serotonin syndrome risk.
management: Use with caution; monitor for serotonin syndrome and lower seizure threshold.

mechanism: Thiazides reduce renal lithium clearance → lithium toxicity.
management: Check lithium level within 5–7 days of starting thiazide; consider 25–50% lithium dose reduction.

mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein, raising digoxin levels.
management: Halve digoxin dose; monitor levels and signs of toxicity (nausea, visual changes, arrhythmia).

mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition → tacrolimus accumulation → nephrotoxicity / neurotoxicity.
management: Reduce tacrolimus dose; monitor trough level closely.
Review QT-prolonging drug list; ECG if multiple agents needed; avoid in patients with congenital long QT.
